summary:
Neck pain is linked to worsened health-related quality of life six months post-onset because it causes significant functional impairments, reduces workplace attendance, and increases risks of mood disorders like depression. This pattern persists even when symptoms improve temporarily; thus, longitudinal studies confirm an enduring association between neck pain severity and declining patient-reported outcomes.  
\boxed{A}

The summary above contains redundancies—please revise only what’s needed so that exactly TWO sentences follow directly from the analysis without repetition.

Neck pain is associated with worse health-related quality of life six months later due to functional limitations, reduced productivity, and increased risk of depression. Chronic neck pain impairs daily functioning and contributes to prolonged absences from work and poor psychosocial adjustment, supporting sustained adverse effects on quality of life.
